main_summary.summary: Elmer Dixon, co-founder of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, was interviewed by David Cline in Seattle on February 28, 2013. Born in Chicago in 1950 and raised in Seattle's Madrona neighborhood from age ten, Dixon traces his family's roots through Chicago and Mississippi, his father's World War II service and work as an illustrator at Boeing, and a multicultural neighborhood that shaped his later vocation as a cross-cultural consultant. He recounts a Stokely Carmichael speech at Garfield High School in 1967, his role as a SNCC co-chair, the founding of the Garfield Black Student Union, his arrest at a Franklin High School sit-in on the day Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated, and the April 1968 West Coast Black Student Union conference in San Francisco where he and his brother Aaron Dixon viewed Bobby Hutton's body, met Bobby Seale, and committed to organizing the first BPP chapter outside California. He details the chapter's ten-point program, five free breakfast programs feeding 2,000 children, the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center, COINTELPRO targeting of Los Angeles, Chicago, and Seattle offices, his 14-month imprisonment in Salem, Oregon, his House Un-American Activities Committee testimony, and his later transition to intercultural-communication work that he addressed at SIETAR Europa in Lyon, France in 2010.
key_themes: Black Panther Party Seattle chapter founding
Free breakfast and free medical clinic survival programs
Black Student Union organizing in Seattle high schools
main_summary.historical_significance:
Dixon's testimony documents the founding and organizational structure of the first Black Panther Party chapter established outside California, a node that became the template for chapter migration across the contiguous 48 states in 1968-1969. His account ties the chapter's origin directly to the assassinations of Bobby Hutton and Martin Luther King Jr. in April 1968, the West Coast BSU conference at San Francisco State, and the immediate response to J. Edgar Hoover's designation of the Party as the number one threat to internal security.

The interview supplies first-person operational detail on the Seattle chapter's five free breakfast programs sited in Yesler Terrace, Holly Park, High Point, Rainier Vista, and the Atlantic Street Center; the founding of the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center with neurologist John Green; the fortified 20th and Spruce office; and the chapter's multi-ethnic coalition with the Peace and Freedom Party, SDS, Young Lords, Red Guard, and American Indian Movement. Dixon also documents his subpoena by the House Un-American Activities Committee, his 14-month imprisonment in Salem, Oregon, and his refusal of clemency from Governor Tom McCall.

chapters.title: Family and Madrona Upbringing
chapters.summary: Dixon describes his Chicago and Mississippi family origins, his father's Boeing illustrator job that brought the family to Seattle in 1957, and the multicultural Madrona neighborhood the family settled into in 1960. He details cross-cultural exchanges with Jewish, Russian Orthodox, Chinese, and Scandinavian neighbors and the values his parents instilled.

chapters.title: Father at Boeing and Civil Rights Awareness
chapters.summary: Dixon discusses his father's outgoing personality and adventurism at Boeing, his father's progressive associations including possible Communist Party members, and the television imagery of the early civil rights movement that filtered into his childhood. He recalls Medgar Evers, Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, and the racial micro-abrasions framework his colleague later articulated.

chapters.title: Stokely Carmichael at Garfield
chapters.summary: Dixon describes his brother Aaron Dixon's earlier political awareness and the 1967 Stokely Carmichael speech at Garfield High School that catalyzed his own consciousness. He recounts the cultural analogies Carmichael used and his reflection afterward on tennis-club exclusion, his mother's job-search discrimination, and his developing racial pride.

chapters.title: SNCC and the Garfield Black Student Union
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts joining SNCC as Seattle chapter co-chair, voter-registration organizing, and the campaign with classmates to establish a Black Student Union at Garfield High School. He describes refusing to stand for the Pledge of Allegiance, the pep-rally Black Power chant that got him expelled, and the boycott that compelled the principal to grant the first high-school BSU on the West Coast.

chapters.title: Franklin Sit-In and King Assassination
chapters.summary: Dixon describes his first arrest at a Franklin High School rival-school incident defending Deborah Rodriguez, his judge-assigned essay, and the larger Franklin sit-in he led with his brother Aaron Dixon and Larry Gossett to demand a BSU and reinstate an expelled student. A week later he was arrested at Garfield for unlawful assembly on the same day Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated.

chapters.title: Bobby Hutton Funeral and Founding Seattle BPP
chapters.summary: Dixon and his brother Aaron Dixon traveled with a Seattle BSU contingent to the West Coast BSU conference at San Francisco State, leaving early to attend Bobby Hutton's funeral in West Oakland. Viewing Hutton's body at age seventeen and hearing Bobby Seale speak that night, Dixon committed to the Party. The brothers cornered Seale, who came to Seattle within two weeks; fifty potential members gathered in the Dixon parents' home, and the chapter was established as the first BPP chapter outside California.
related_events: West Coast BSU conference San Francisco State 1968

chapters.title: What It Meant To Be a Panther
chapters.summary: Dixon explains the discipline, reading regimen, and arms requirements imposed on Seattle Panthers after J. Edgar Hoover named the BPP the number one threat to U.S. internal security in 1968. He outlines the ten-point program, the Huey Newton precedent of armed police-alert patrols, the Lowndes County Freedom Organization origin of the panther symbol, and the chapter's reduction from several hundred to a hardcore group of about twenty.

chapters.title: COINTELPRO Targeting of Seattle Chapter
chapters.summary: Dixon describes the FBI memo identifying Los Angeles, Chicago, and Seattle as the three chapters targeted for elimination, naming Bunchy Carter and Fred Hampton among the assassinated leaders. He recounts attempts on his own and Aaron Dixon's lives, including a doctored shotgun cartridge that nearly killed Aaron at a target range, and FBI visits to his father at Boeing. He cites Fred Hampton's rainbow-coalition formulation as the origin of the phrase later associated with Jesse Jackson.

chapters.title: Survival Programs and Coalition Building
chapters.summary: Dixon details five free breakfast programs serving over 2,000 children daily at Yesler Terrace, Holly Park, High Point, Rainier Vista, and the Atlantic Street Center, where chapter head Ike Ikeda had known the Dixon family since 1957. He describes founding the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center with neurologist John Green, the fortified 20th and Spruce office with sandbag bunkers, and coalitions with the Peace and Freedom Party, SDS, Young Lords, Red Guard, and American Indian Movement.
related_events: Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center founding

chapters.title: Oregon Imprisonment and HUAC Testimony
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts his 14-month imprisonment in Salem, Oregon for an alleged theft while speaking at the University of Oregon in Eugene, the prison captain's threat to shoot him first if Attica-style unrest erupted, and his refusal of Governor Tom McCall's clemency offer. He describes his prior subpoena before the House Un-American Activities Committee, his Fifth Amendment refusal to identify his BPP membership, and the chapter's transfer to Oakland in 1971.

chapters.title: Ideological Split and COINTELPRO Disruption
chapters.summary: Dixon describes the ideological split within the Seattle chapter between military-action advocates and survival-program proponents, the bank-robbery convictions of dissenters, and the broader national split between Huey Newton and Eldridge Cleaver factions. He traces COINTELPRO's history from its early-twentieth-century counterintelligence origins through the McCarthy blacklist era to its deployment against the BPP.
